hook: A Saadian royal necropolis walled up by a rival sultan and forgotten for two centuries, until an aerial survey spotted it from the air in 1917.
facts: Royal necropolis of the Saadian dynasty, with the main mausoleum built under Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur in the late 16th century beside the Kasbah Mosque.
The Alaouite Sultan Moulay Ismail sealed off the access in the early 18th century to erase his predecessors' memory, rather than desecrate the graves.
The site lay forgotten until 1917, when a French aerial survey of Marrakech revealed it and a passage was reopened.
The 'Hall of Twelve Columns' over the tomb of al-Mansur's son uses Carrara marble and finely carved cedar and stucco.
w: Set on erasing the Saadians who ruled before him, Moulay Ismail could not bring himself to disturb their dead -- so he simply walled up their necropolis and left it to be forgotten. Two centuries on, in 1917, a French aerial survey caught the buried courtyards from the air. Marble, cedar and twelve columns, sealed in plain sight.

hook: The skeleton of a once-incomparable Saadian palace, stripped to its mud-brick bones and now ruled by nesting storks.
facts: Commissioned by Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur from 1578, partly financed by ransoms paid for Portuguese prisoners taken at the Battle of the Three Kings.
Its name derives from al-Badi, 'the Incomparable', one of the ninety-nine names of God in the Quran.
The Alaouite Moulay Ismail spent over a decade from the 1690s stripping its marble, gold and fittings to build his new capital at Meknes.
White storks nest along the ruined ramparts, clattering their bills loudly in the spring breeding season.
w: Al-Mansur named it 'the Incomparable' and paid for it with ransom gold wrung from Portuguese prisoners, importing marble from Italy. A century later Moulay Ismail stripped it bare to build Meknes, leaving these vast sunken courts and towering pise walls. Storks rule the ramparts now, clattering their bills over the wreck.

hook: Marrakech's oldest standing monument: a lone Almoravid ablution pavilion that survived nine centuries by being buried under the street.
facts: Built around 1117 to 1125 under the Almoravid emir Ali ibn Yusuf, the city's only surviving Almoravid-era building.
Served as a ritual ablution kiosk for worshippers at the adjacent Ben Youssef Mosque.
Excavated by French archaeologists in 1948 and now sits roughly three metres below modern street level.
Its dome interior carries an intricate vocabulary of pine cones, palms and acanthus, and some of the oldest cursive Maghrebi inscriptions in North Africa.
w: Every later dynasty razed what the Almoravids built -- except this small domed kiosk, which survived by sinking. Carved with pine cones, palms and the oldest cursive script in the Maghreb, it once supplied ablution water to the mosque next door. Dug out only in 1948, it sits three metres down. The city's oldest stone, saved by burial.

hook: A 16th-century Quranic college where 900 students once crammed into 130 tiny cells around a pool of carved cedar and zellij.
facts: The present madrasa was commissioned by the Saadian sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib in the 16th century, on a site first endowed by Ali ibn Yusuf.
Among the largest theological colleges in North Africa, said to have housed up to 900 students in around 130 dormitory cells.
Taught the Quran and religious sciences for over five centuries before closing in the 1960s.
The marble-and-cedar courtyard centres on a shallow reflecting pool framed by stucco and zellij.
w: For five centuries up to 900 students lived here, packed into 130 cells barely bigger than cupboards, climbing down to a courtyard of cedar, marble and zellij to learn the Quran by heart. The cells are still there, low and claustrophobic, ranged above a still reflecting pool. A whole university folded into one carved courtyard.

hook: A 16th-century Saadian wall-fountain with a carved cedar canopy: one basin for people, two for their animals, charity made architecture.
facts: Part of the Mouassine Mosque complex built for the Saadian sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib between roughly 1562 and 1573.
One of the largest fountains in the medina, around 18 metres long, with a projecting carved-wood canopy over a corbelled arch.
Of its four arches, one trough served people and two served animals, fed from the complex's water system.
In Islamic tradition giving water to drink ranks among the most honourable forms of alms, hence the fountain beside the mosque.
w: Eighteen metres of carved cedar canopy and corbelled arch, built so a Saadian sultan could turn charity into stone: in Islam, giving water to drink counts among the highest alms. Of its four troughs, one slaked people and two slaked their mules and donkeys. Easy to walk straight past now -- a public tap dressed as a monument.

hook: A single-storey labyrinth of courtyards built by a grand vizier too heavy for stairs, named for his favourite wife.
facts: Begun by the vizier Si Musa in the 1860s and vastly expanded by his son, Grand Vizier Ba Ahmad ibn Musa, between 1894 and 1900.
Named al-Bahia, 'the Brilliant', reportedly after Ba Ahmad's favourite wife.
Built entirely on one level, said in part to spare the stout vizier the climbing of stairs.
Its Carrara-marble Grand Courtyard measures roughly 50 by 30 metres; some 80 rooms are linked to the harem and concubines.
w: A grand vizier named it 'the Brilliant' after his favourite wife and, by one account, built it all on one floor because his bulk made stairs a misery. Painted cedar ceilings, a marble court of fifty paces, eighty rooms trailing into the old harem. When he died, the sultan's men stripped it within days. Power, briefly, in zellij.

hook: A war minister's mansion seized by the brutal Pasha Glaoui while its owner was abroad, now a museum under a vast metal chandelier.
facts: Housed in Dar Mnebhi, built in the early 20th century by Mehdi al-Mnebhi, war minister to Sultan Moulay Abdelaziz.
The palace was seized by the family of Pasha Thami El Glaoui while Mnebhi served as ambassador in London.
Used as a girls' school after independence, then restored and opened as a museum by the Omar Benjelloun Foundation in 1997.
The covered central court hangs a very large chandelier of pierced metal plates over zellij and stucco; the old hammam survives.
w: While war minister al-Mnebhi played ambassador in London, the all-powerful Pasha Glaoui simply helped himself to his Marrakech mansion. It became a girls' school, then a museum. The covered court now drips with stalactite stucco under a colossal pierced-metal chandelier; the old hammam survives. A stolen palace, beautifully kept.

hook: The only city gate named for a trade, opening onto stone vats where hides have been cured the same pungent medieval way for 900 years.
facts: Bab Debbagh, 'Gate of the Tanners', dates to the Almoravid walls of around 1126 and is the only Marrakech gate named after a craft.
Tanners are said to have been among the city's first settlers, sited east near the seasonal Oued Issil for the water tanning needs.
Hides are still soaked and softened in pits using natural agents, traditionally including lime and pigeon droppings rich in ammonia.
The gate has the most convoluted plan of any in the city, its passage bending five times through courts and a vaulted chamber.
w: Of nineteen gates only one bears a trade's name: the tanners', who reputedly settled Marrakech first and have soaked hides in these stone pits ever since, using lime and ammonia-rich pigeon dung. The stench arrives before you do. The gate itself bends five times like a trap. Nine centuries of leather, cured the medieval way.

hook: The blue-tiled Lazama synagogue, founded by Jews fleeing the Spanish expulsion of 1492 and still tucked inside a Mellah courtyard.
facts: Founded in 1492 by Sephardic Jews fleeing the Edict of Expulsion from Spain; the present building dates to the 20th century.
The name Lazama derives from al-Azma, referring to those who 'ran away' from Spain.
Built in riad form, turning inward around a courtyard, in the heart of the Mellah established in 1558.
Now the main synagogue of the Mellah and the one reliably open to visitors, as the city's once-large Jewish community has dwindled.
w: Its very name, Lazama, means the runaways -- the Jews who fled Ferdinand and Isabella's Spain in 1492 and carried their rite south to Marrakech. The prayer hall hides riad-style behind a blue-tiled courtyard in the Mellah. Marrakech once held tens of thousands of Jews; this remains the synagogue still reliably lit and open.

hook: A vast field of whitewashed tombs on the medina's edge, including a corner for thousands of children lost to a single typhus epidemic.
facts: The largest Jewish cemetery in Morocco, its core dating to the 16th century when the Mellah was established in 1558.
Whitewashed graves with rounded tops carry Hebrew inscriptions; tombs of Kohanim are marked with blue paint.
One corner is given over to roughly 6,000 children who died in a 19th-century typhus epidemic.
Revered rabbis lie here, among them Abraham Azulai, a focus of pilgrimage and prayer.
w: Rows on rows of low, whitewashed, round-topped tombs run to the medina wall -- the largest Jewish cemetery in Morocco. Priestly graves wear a wash of blue. In one corner lie some 6,000 children carried off by a single typhus epidemic in the 1800s. Pilgrims still come to pray over the old rabbis. A whole community, written in lime.

hook: A restored merchants' inn hung with vintage prints of a vanished Morocco: caravans before trucks, mountain villages before roads.
facts: Opened in 2009 in a renovated funduq near the Ben Youssef Madrasa by collectors Patrick Menac'h and Hamid Mergani.
Drawn from a private archive of some 60,000 images, it shows original photographs taken between 1870 and 1950.
Includes work by named photographers like Marcelin Flandrin alongside anonymous studies of Moroccan daily life.
A rooftop terrace and a small cinema screening 1950s documentary film crown the museum.
w: Two collectors spent years hunting vintage prints of a Morocco that no longer exists -- Saharan caravans before trucks, Atlas villages before roads, tribal portraits in studio light -- and hung the best of 60,000 images in this old merchants' inn near the madrasa. Climb the roof terrace for tea over the medina. A lost country, framed.

hook: One Dutch traveller's lifetime hoard of Saharan textiles and jewellery, arranged as a journey down the old caravan road to Timbuktu.
facts: Built around the collection of Dutch anthropologist Bert Flint, who settled in Marrakech in 1957 and lived in the house until his death in 2022.
Set in his early-20th-century riad on Rue de la Bahia, between the Bahia and Dar Si Said palaces.
The display traces the trans-Saharan 'gold road' from Marrakech to Timbuktu through carpets, costume and adornment.
Opened as a museum in 1996; rooms are themed by the peoples along the caravan routes.
w: A Dutchman fell for Morocco in 1957, never really left, and spent six decades amassing the textiles, silver and amber of the Sahara. He hung it all through his own riad as a journey down the caravan road from Marrakech to Timbuktu, room by room. Bert Flint died here in 2022, aged 91. A private obsession you can walk through.

hook: A defence minister's painted-cedar palace, now Morocco's national museum of carpets and weaving, around a riad garden pavilion.
facts: Built 1894 to 1900 by Si Sa'id ibn Musa, defence minister and brother of Grand Vizier Ba Ahmad.
Converted to a museum of Moroccan crafts around 1930, and reopened in 2018 as the National Museum of Weaving and Carpets.
Centres on a riad garden with a painted-wood pavilion and a grand upstairs reception hall.
Significantly damaged in the September 2023 earthquake and closed for repairs.
w: The defence minister built it beside his brother the grand vizier, all painted cedar and a green riad court with a wooden pavilion. Now it holds Morocco's national collection of carpets -- the knotted geography of every region in one palace. The 2023 quake shook it hard and shut it. Faded power, hung with wool.

hook: A green-roofed pavilion mirrored in a vast 12th-century irrigation basin, fringed with olives and the legends of drowned lovers.
facts: An olive grove and reservoir laid out under the Almohads in the 12th century, fed from the Atlas to irrigate the plantation.
The green-tiled pavilion beside the basin dates in its present form to the 19th century, under the Alaouite sultans.
Sultan Mohammed IV died here in 1873 when his steam launch capsized on the basin.
The Atlas snows reflect in the water on clear days, a postcard staple of Marrakech.
legend: Legend says a sultan drowned unfaithful or discarded concubines in the basin by night, throwing them from the pavilion balcony.
w: Under the olives the Almohads dug this huge tank eight centuries ago to water the grove; the little green-roofed pavilion came later. They say a sultan drowned discarded lovers here by night, tipping them from the balcony -- and a real sultan did die here in 1873 when his steam launch capsized. The Atlas snows float on the water.

hook: A quiet colonial-era graveyard in the new town where French settlers, Free French soldiers and stray foreign souls of every faith lie together.
facts: Laid out in Gueliz on Rue Erraouda, dating from around 1925, for the city's European residents.
Holds a military section for Free French and Allied dead of the Second World War, marked each 11 November.
Despite the name, it mixes Catholic, Protestant and Orthodox graves with Buddhist, Shinto and unmarked agnostic ones.
Replaced an earlier European burial ground inside the medina that no longer survives.
w: Behind a wall in the orderly new town lie the people the medina never claimed: French settlers, Free French soldiers fallen in 1940s campaigns, and a scatter of foreigners of every creed -- Catholic, Orthodox, Buddhist, Shinto, godless. Cypress, dust and faded enamel portraits. Each November a small crowd still gathers for the war dead.

hook: The shrine of the patron of the blind, greatest of the seven saints whose week-long pilgrimage spirals counter-clockwise around the city.
facts: Tomb-shrine of Sidi Bel Abbes Sebti, born in Ceuta in 1130, the most venerated of the Seven Saints of Marrakech.
He was a noted patron of the poor and especially the blind; food is still distributed to the needy at the zaouia.
The Seven Saints pilgrimage, or ziyara, was organised by Sultan Moulay Ismail in the late 17th century.
Its week-long circuit of the seven tombs runs counter-clockwise, echoing the circling of the Kaaba in Mecca.
legend: They say Sidi Bel Abbes waited forty years at the city gates without entering, coming in only at the sultan's own invitation.
w: Greatest of the seven saints who guard Marrakech, Sidi Bel Abbes fed the poor and the blind, who still gather at his door for bread. Legend says he camped forty years outside the walls, refusing to enter until a sultan begged him. The week-long pilgrimage of the seven tombs spirals counter-clockwise round the city, mirroring the Kaaba.

hook: Twin riad gardens fed by an ancient underground khettara, derelict for decades and only coaxed back to life in 2016.
facts: Set on the footprint of a Saadian-era complex begun in the 16th century in the Mouassine district, later rebuilt in the 19th.
Once owned by al-Hajj Muhammad Loukrissi, former head of the watchmakers' guild and a sultan's chamberlain, who died here in 1934.
It then fell into ruin until a restoration begun in 2008, opening to the public in 2016.
Water still arrives through a khettara, an underground channel tapping the water table, feeding the fountains and Islamic-style garden.
w: Behind an anonymous Mouassine wall sit two formal riad gardens on Saadian foundations, once owned by the head of the city's watchmakers' guild. After he died in 1934 the place rotted for decades. Water still reaches it the old way, by a khettara tunnelled to the water table. Reopened in 2016 -- a courtyard the souk had quietly forgotten.

hook: An 18th-century prince's pleasure orchard that the French turned botanical and the telecoms age wired with free internet terminals.
facts: Originally a royal garden, Arsat Moulay Abdeslam, given to a prince by Sultan Sidi Mohamed ben Abdullah in the 18th century.
The French reworked the food-producing orchard into a botanical garden during the Protectorate.
Restored by UNESCO and Maroc Telecom and reopened in 2005 as Morocco's first 'Cyber Park', with terminals and Wi-Fi.
Roughly eight hectares of olives, palms and citrus laid out on Islamic garden principles, just off Avenue Mohammed V.
w: A prince got this walled orchard as a wedding gift in the 1700s; the French made it botanical; then in 2005 the phone company bolted internet terminals among the olives and renamed it the Cyber Park. It reads oddly well -- citrus, palms and water channels laid to old Islamic geometry, with kiosks and Wi-Fi. Eight hectares of quiet off the boulevard.

hook: The old apothecaries' square where herbalists still sell dried chameleons and roots for love spells alongside the saffron and cumin.
facts: A medina square trading for centuries as a grain market and, historically, a slave-auction site.
Now Marrakech's spice and herbalist square, heaped with cumin, saffron, dried rosebuds and argan oil.
Stalls also sell ingredients used in traditional folk medicine and reputed magic: dried chameleons, hedgehog skins and unnamed roots.
Café des Epices, opened on its edge in 2000, gives a rooftop view over the whole square.
legend: Vendors here are widely said to supply the ingredients of love charms and curses prepared by local sorcerers.
w: Once a grain market and slave block, this square now runs on saffron, cumin and rosebuds -- and, on the shadier stalls, the dried chameleons, hedgehog skins and unnamed roots said to feed local love charms and curses. Herbalists weigh out hammam soap and kohl beside the magic. Climb the Cafe des Epices roof and watch the trade below.
